feynman.0: The moment a body leaves your hand, the bat, or the launch ramp, there’s only one thing pushing on it — gravity, and nothing else. And the whole business of analysing projectiles falls right out of a single idea: the sideways motion and the up-and-down motion are completely independent, and only the up-and-down part ever accelerates.
feynman.1: Newton’s laws don’t change one bit when two or more forces act on a body in the same plane. The net force is just the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration follows Fnet = ma pointing the way that net force points. Now, when the forces don’t lie along the same line, here’s the trick: you replace each force by its components along two perpendicular axes, add up the components separately, and then treat each axis as its own little one-dimensional problem — two easy problems instead of one hard one.
feynman.2: Take a force F pointing at some angle θ to an axis you’ve picked. You split it into a piece F cosθ running along that axis and a piece F sinθ at right angles to it. You’re free to point the axes any way you like — but a clever choice saves you work: line them up so that as many forces as possible already sit right along them.
feynman.3: The inclined plane is the classic two-dimensional force problem — the one everybody cuts their teeth on. Picture a block of mass m sitting on a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, only two forces are at work: its weight mg pulling straight down, and the normal force N pushing out perpendicular to the slope’s surface. Since the block can only ever slide along the slope, we point our axes parallel and perpendicular to the slope — and then it’s the weight we break up into those axes.
feynman.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block doesn’t go anywhere, so the forces there must cancel: N = mg cosθ. Along the slope, the only force left unbalanced is the bit of weight pointing down the slope, mg sinθ, so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope works out to be:
feynman.5: Notice the mass has dropped right out — the acceleration doesn’t care how heavy the block is. And the two extremes check it for us: a flat surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and no acceleration at all, while a sheer drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g, which is just free fall again. Draw yourself a free-body diagram before you touch a single number — it’s the surest way to keep from resolving the wrong force, as in Figure 1.1.
feynman.6: Trap On an inclined plane the normal force is N = mg cosθ, not mg — don’t fall for it. The full weight only presses perpendicular to a flat surface; tip the surface and only the perpendicular slice mg cosθ pushes into it, while the parallel slice mg sinθ goes to shoving the block down the slope. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
feynman.7: Connects This same resolve-into-axes trick runs every problem in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems over in the circular-motion chapter split a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical pieces in exactly the same way.
feynman.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once we agree to ignore the air. The instant you let it go, the only force on it is its weight mg, straight down. So by Newton’s second law its acceleration is g = 9.8 m s⁻² straight down — and it stays exactly that, same size and same direction, the whole flight long: at launch, at the very top of the arc, and at every spot in between.
feynman.9: Here’s what makes the model so powerful: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and you can take them apart and handle them on their own. There’s no horizontal force, so the horizontal velocity just stays put for the whole flight. The vertical motion is plain free fall with uniform acceleration — and if you launch horizontally, it’s identical to simply dropping the thing from rest. A ball thrown sideways and a ball dropped from the same height at the same moment hit the floor together, because their vertical motions are the same and the horizontal motion doesn’t touch them.
feynman.10: Key idea All through a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is a steady 9.8 m s⁻² straight down. The horizontal velocity holds constant; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ every second. The two motions are independent and share just one thing — the time of flight.
feynman.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are tied together by a single quantity, and only one: the time. The very same elapsed time turns up in both the horizontal and the vertical equations — and that’s exactly the hook that lets you solve one motion for the time and then hand that time over to the other. You apply the constant-acceleration equations to each axis on its own, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 horizontally (so along the horizontal s = uxt):
feynman.12: Before you substitute anything, nail down a sign convention and stick with it for the whole problem. Either you call up positive (and then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or you call up negative (and then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). Either way you get the same physical answer — what you mustn’t do is mix the two inside one problem. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal gets split right at the start into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and you carry those two pieces through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
feynman.13: Trap A projectile heading upward is still being pulled downward. While it climbs, the only force on it is still gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole time — even right at the top, where the vertical velocity is momentarily zero. Going up doesn’t require an upward force; the upward velocity is simply being eaten away by that steady downward acceleration. There’s no upward force at any point in the flight.
feynman.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy way of thinking come together in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be dug out from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh held constant, and you get the very same answer the kinematics gives you.
feynman.15: You solve a complete projectile problem like this: split the launch velocity into components, work each axis on its own, and use the shared time of flight to knot them back together. The worked example coming up finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height, and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are drawn in Figure 1.2.
feynman.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and we’re ignoring air resistance. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Keep the full unrounded values in the calculator and round only the final answers, to three significant figures.
feynman.17: Formula: split the launch velocity into components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
feynman.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
feynman.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball comes back to the height it started at, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, which gives t = 2uyg.
feynman.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there’s no acceleration at all, so R = uxt.
feynman.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
feynman.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component hasn’t changed, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The size is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
feynman.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (that is, 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
feynman.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed comes out equal to the launch speed and the landing angle equal to the launch angle — and that’s no accident: it’s the symmetry of a projectile that launches and lands at the same height.
feynman.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: split the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to get the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; put vx and vy back together with Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the one and only bridge between the two axes.
feynman.32: Trap The maximum height happens when the vertical velocity is zero, not when the speed is zero — those aren’t the same thing. At the top of the arc the horizontal velocity ux is still going full strength, so the ball is most definitely still moving, and its speed is ux, not zero. Setting the whole speed to zero at the top is a mistake people make all the time.
feynman.33: Trap Carry the full unrounded values through the whole calculation and round only at the very end. Plug in a value you’ve already chopped to three significant figures — say, using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — and the rounding errors pile up and can nudge your reported time, range and height in that last significant figure.
feynman.34: Everything above ignored the air, and that’s a fine approximation for dense, slow, compact things over short flights. But for light objects, high speeds, or long flights, you can no longer just wave the air away — and for the VCE study all you need is a qualitative picture of what it does.
feynman.35: Air resistance is a force that always fights the motion — it points exactly opposite to the velocity at every instant. And since the velocity runs along the path and keeps swinging around in direction, the air-resistance force keeps swinging around too, and its size grows as the speed grows. So it’s not a constant force at all, and the nice constant-acceleration equations simply stop working.
feynman.36: Here’s the qualitative story of what follows. Air resistance drains energy out of the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range come out smaller than the ideal parabola would give. The path turns lopsided: the coming-down part is steeper than the going-up part, so the projectile covers less ground on the way down than on the way up. The horizontal velocity no longer holds constant — it bleeds away all through the flight, because air resistance has a horizontal piece pushing back against the horizontal motion. The landing speed comes in slower than the launch speed, and the thing hits the ground at a steeper angle than it left at.
feynman.37: As a falling object picks up speed, the upward air-resistance force grows and grows until it just matches the weight. At that point the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the object keeps right on at a steady terminal velocity. A projectile sent off on a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed instead of speeding up forever. The qualitative picture is gathered together in Figure 1.3.
feynman.38: Trap Air resistance doesn’t only act up and down. It opposes the whole velocity, so it’s got a horizontal piece too — and that’s precisely why the horizontal velocity no longer stays constant and the range shrinks. An answer that keeps the horizontal velocity constant while “adding drag” only to the vertical motion has got it wrong.
feynman.39: Connects Terminal velocity is just the velocity where the resistive force balances the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning we use for a charged particle settling to a constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses all through this area of study.

sagan.0: The moment a body leaves the hand, the bat, or the launch ramp, every push and grip and contact falls away, and gravity alone remains to guide it. The whole of projectile analysis unfolds from a single, luminous idea: the horizontal and vertical motions move in perfect independence of one another, and of the two, only the vertical motion ever quickens.
sagan.1: Newton’s laws hold unchanged when two or more coplanar forces press upon a body at once. The net force is simply the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration follows faithfully along its direction, obeying Fnet = ma. When the forces refuse to lie along a single line, we replace each one by its components along two perpendicular axes, gather the components separately, and then treat the two axes as two independent one-dimensional worlds, each solved in its own right.
sagan.2: A force F pointing at an angle θ to a chosen axis is resolved into a component F cosθ running along that axis and a component F sinθ standing perpendicular to it. We are free to choose our axes however we wish, but a wise choice spares us labour: we align them so that as many forces as possible already lie comfortably along them.
sagan.3: The inclined plane is the classic two-dimensional force problem, and a fine place to learn the method. A block of mass m rests upon a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, only two forces touch it: its weight mg pulling straight down toward the centre of the Earth, and the normal force N pushing outward, perpendicular to the surface on which it lies. Because the block can move only along the slope, we choose our axes parallel and perpendicular to that slope, and it is the weight — not the normal force — that we resolve into them.
sagan.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block goes nowhere, neither sinking into the surface nor leaping from it, so there the forces hold in perfect balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope a single unbalanced force remains, the component of weight drawing the block downward, mg sinθ, and so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
sagan.5: Remarkably, this acceleration takes no notice whatever of the block’s mass. Two limiting cases confirm what we have found: a horizontal surface (θ = 0) yields N = mg and no acceleration at all, while a sheer vertical drop (θ = 90°) yields N = 0 and a = g, returning us to the pure free fall from which we began. A free-body diagram, drawn before a single number is written, is the surest guard against resolving the wrong force, as shown in Figure 1.1.
sagan.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight presses straight down only against a horizontal surface; on a slope, only the perpendicular component mg cosθ bears into the surface, while the parallel component mg sinθ urges the block down the incline. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
sagan.7: Connects This same resolve-into-axes method quietly drives every problem in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in precisely this way.
sagan.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once we agree to set air resistance aside. From the very instant of release, the only force that touches it is its weight mg, pulling straight down toward the Earth. By Newton’s second law its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward, unwavering in both magnitude and direction through the whole of its flight — at the launch, at the highest point of its arc, and at every place between.
sagan.9: The quiet power of this model lies in a single fact: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and may be analysed each on its own. There is no horizontal force, so the horizontal velocity holds perfectly steady from launch to landing. The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated free fall, no different from a body simply let go from rest, if the launch is horizontal. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same moment reach the ground together — for their vertical motions are one and the same, untouched by whatever happens horizontally.
sagan.10: Key idea Throughout a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward. The horizontal velocity never changes; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ with every passing second. The two motions are independent, and share but one thing between them: the time of flight.
sagan.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are bound together by one quantity alone: the time. The very same elapsed time appears in both the horizontal and the vertical equations, and it is this that lets us solve one motion for the time and then carry that time across into the other. The constant-acceleration equations are applied separately to each axis, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so horizontally s = uxt):
sagan.12: A consistent sign convention must be settled before a single value is substituted, and then held faithfully for the whole of the problem. Either up is taken as positive (and then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or up is taken as negative (and then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices arrive at the same physical answer; mixing them within one problem does not. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is resolved at the very outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are then carried, each through its own axis, independently.
sagan.13: Trap A projectile rising upward is still pulled downward. As it climbs, the only force upon it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole way — even at the very summit, where the vertical velocity falls for one instant to zero. Upward motion demands no upward force; the upward velocity is merely being eaten away by the constant downward acceleration. At no stage of the flight is there any upward force at all.
sagan.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach meet in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh held constant, giving the very same result as the kinematic method.
sagan.15: A complete projectile problem is solved by resolving the launch velocity into its components, analysing each axis on its own, and using the shared time of flight to draw the two together. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height, and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The graceful parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.

Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so that a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. We keep the unrounded intermediate values in the calculator and round only the final answers, to three significant figures.
sagan.17: Formula: resolve the launch velocity into its components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
sagan.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
sagan.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball returns to the height from which it began, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, giving t = 2uyg.
sagan.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there is no acceleration, so R = uxt.
sagan.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top of the arc the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
sagan.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component is unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
sagan.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (that is, 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
sagan.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed comes back equal to the launch speed and the landing angle equal to the launch angle — a quiet confirmation of the deep symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at the same height.
sagan.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy by Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single thread linking the two axes.
sagan.32: Trap The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity is zero, not when the speed is zero. At the very top of the path the horizontal velocity ux sweeps on undiminished, so the ball is still very much in motion and its speed is ux, not zero. Setting the whole speed to zero at the top is a common and tempting error.
sagan.33: Trap Carry the unrounded intermediate values through the calculation and round only at the very end. Substituting a value already rounded to three significant figures — using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation, say — lets rounding error accumulate and can nudge the reported time, range, and height in their final significant figure.
sagan.34: The analysis above sets air resistance aside, which is an excellent approximation for dense, slow, compact objects over short flights. For light objects, for high speeds, or for long flights, air resistance can no longer be wished away, and here the VCE study asks only for a qualitative description of what it does.
sagan.35: Air resistance is a force that forever opposes the motion — at every instant it points exactly against the velocity. Because the velocity lies tangent to the path and turns continually as the body arcs through the air, the air-resistance force turns with it, never holding a fixed direction, and its magnitude swells as the speed grows. It is therefore no constant force, and the simple constant-acceleration equations can no longer carry us.
sagan.36: Its consequences we describe qualitatively, as follows. Air resistance steadily drains energy from the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range are diminished against the ideal parabola. The path itself grows asymmetric: the descent falls more steeply than the ascent rose, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did going up. The horizontal velocity no longer holds steady — it dwindles throughout the flight, for air resistance now has a horizontal component set against the horizontal motion. The landing speed comes in below the launch speed, and the projectile meets the ground at a steeper angle than the one at which it left.
sagan.37: As a falling object gathers speed, the upward air-resistance force grows and grows until at last it balances the weight. In that moment the net force vanishes, the acceleration falls to zero, and the object sails on at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile cast into a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed rather than quickening without end. The qualitative picture is gathered together in Figure 1.3.
sagan.38: Trap Air resistance does not act in the vertical alone. It opposes the whole velocity, so it carries a horizontal component too — which is precisely why the horizontal velocity no longer holds steady and the range is shortened. A response that keeps the horizontal velocity constant while “adding drag” to the vertical motion alone is incorrect.
sagan.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force comes into balance with the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning we use for a charged particle settling to a constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses throughout this area of study.

curie.0: Once a body leaves the hand, the bat or the launch ramp, gravity is the only force acting on it. The whole of projectile analysis follows from a single idea, and I hold to it throughout: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and only the vertical motion accelerates.
curie.1: Newton’s laws apply unchanged when two or more coplanar forces act on a body. The net force is the vector sum of the forces, and the acceleration obeys Fnet = ma in the direction of that net force. When the forces are not collinear, I replace each force by its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components separately, and treat the two axes as two independent one-dimensional problems.
curie.2: A force F directed at an angle θ to a chosen axis is resolved into a component F cosθ along that axis and a component F sinθ perpendicular to it. The choice of axes is free, but a careful choice removes labour: I align the axes so that as many forces as possible already lie along them.
curie.3: The inclined plane is the standard two-dimensional force problem. A block of mass m rests on a slope inclined at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, two forces act on it: its weight mg directed vertically downward, and the normal force N directed perpendicular to the slope surface. Because the block can accelerate only along the slope, I choose the axes parallel and perpendicular to the slope, and it is the weight that is resolved into these axes.
curie.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block does not accelerate, so the forces balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope the only unbalanced force is the component of weight down the slope, mg sinθ, so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
curie.5: The acceleration is independent of the block’s mass. The two limits confirm the result: a horizontal surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and zero acceleration, and a vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g, recovering free fall. A free-body diagram drawn before any calculation is the surest defence against resolving the wrong force, as shown in Figure 1.1.
curie.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight acts only perpendicular to a horizontal surface; on a slope, only the perpendicular component mg cosθ presses into the surface, while the parallel component mg sinθ drives the block down the slope. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
curie.7: Connects The same resolve-into-axes method drives every problem in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in exactly this way.
curie.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once air resistance is ignored. The instant the body is released, the only force acting on it is its weight mg, directed vertically downward. By Newton’s second law its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward, constant in both magnitude and direction throughout the flight — at the launch, at the top of the path, and at every point between.
curie.9: The power of the model lies in this: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent and may be analysed separately. There is no horizontal force, so the horizontal velocity is constant throughout the flight. The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated free fall, identical to the motion of a body simply dropped from rest if the launch is horizontal. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same instant strike the ground together, because their vertical motions are identical and unaffected by the horizontal motion. This I take to be the heart of the matter.
curie.10: Key idea Throughout a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward. Horizontal velocity is constant; vertical velocity changes at 9.8 m s⁻¹ each second. The two motions are independent and share only the time of flight.
curie.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are linked by one quantity alone: the time. The same elapsed time appears in both the horizontal and the vertical equations, and this is what allows one motion to be solved for the time and that time then fed into the other. I apply the constant-acceleration equations separately to each axis, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so horizontally s = uxt):
curie.12: A consistent sign convention must be fixed before any substitution and held for the entire problem. Either up is taken as positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or up is taken as negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices give the same physical answer; mixing them within one problem does not. I resolve a launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal at the outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and carry these two components through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
curie.13: Trap A projectile moving upward is still pulled downward. As it rises, the only force on it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole time — even at the very top, where the vertical velocity is momentarily zero. Upward motion does not require an upward force; the upward velocity is simply being reduced by the constant downward acceleration. There is no upward force at any stage of the flight.
curie.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach meet in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh constant, giving the same result as the kinematic method.
curie.15: A complete projectile problem is solved by resolving the launch velocity into components, analysing each axis separately, and using the shared time of flight to connect them. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
curie.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is ignored. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. I retain unrounded intermediate values in the calculator and round only the final answers, to three significant figures.

curie.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there is no acceleration, so R = uxt.
curie.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
curie.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component is unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.

curie.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy by Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. Substituting a value already rounded to three significant figures — for example using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — accumulates rounding error and can shift the reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.

greene.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy by Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single link between the two axes.
greene.32: Trap The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity falls to zero—not when the speed falls to zero. At the top of the arc the horizontal velocity ux sails on undiminished, so the ball is still very much in motion, its speed being ux, not zero. Setting the full speed to zero at the top is a common and tempting error.
greene.33: Trap Carry unrounded intermediate values through the whole calculation and round only the final answer. Slip in a value already rounded to three significant figures—using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation, say—and rounding error quietly accumulates, enough to nudge the reported time, range and height in their final significant figure.
greene.34: Everything above quietly set air resistance aside, and for dense, slow, compact objects over short flights that is an excellent approximation indeed. But for light objects, high speeds or long flights, air resistance can no longer be brushed away—and here the VCE study asks only for a qualitative description of what it does.
greene.35: Air resistance is a force that always opposes the motion—at every instant it points in exactly the direction opposite to the velocity. Because the velocity runs tangent to the path and is forever changing direction, the air-resistance force wheels around with it throughout the flight, and its magnitude swells as the speed grows. It is therefore no constant force at all, and the tidy constant-acceleration equations no longer hold.
greene.36: The consequences can be sketched qualitatively. Air resistance drains energy from the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range shrink compared with the ideal parabola. The path turns asymmetric: the descent is steeper than the ascent, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did going up. The horizontal velocity no longer holds steady—it bleeds away throughout the flight, because air resistance carries a horizontal component opposing the horizontal motion. The landing speed falls short of the launch speed, and the projectile meets the ground at a steeper angle than the one at which it left.
greene.37: As a falling object gathers speed, the upward air-resistance force grows and grows until at last it matches the weight. At that moment the net force vanishes, the acceleration drops to zero, and the object glides on at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile thrown into a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed rather than accelerating without end. The qualitative picture is gathered together in Figure 1.3.
greene.38: Trap Air resistance does not act only vertically. It opposes the whole velocity, so it carries a horizontal component too—which is precisely why the horizontal velocity no longer holds constant and the range is reduced. A response that keeps the horizontal velocity fixed while “adding drag” to the vertical motion alone has missed the point.
greene.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force comes into balance with the weight—the very same force-balance reasoning we use for a charged particle settling to constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses threaded throughout this area of study.

hawking.0: Let go of the ball, the bat, the launch ramp, and one force remains: gravity. Everything in projectile analysis follows from a single idea. The horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and only the vertical one accelerates.
hawking.1: Newton’s laws hold unchanged when two or more coplanar forces act on a body. The net force is the vector sum of them, and the acceleration follows Fnet = ma in the direction of that net force. When the forces do not lie along one line, replace each by its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components separately, and treat the two axes as two independent one-dimensional problems.
hawking.2: A force F at angle θ to a chosen axis splits into a component F cosθ along that axis and a component F sinθ across it. You may choose the axes freely. A good choice saves work: align them so that as many forces as possible already lie along them.
hawking.3: The inclined plane is the standard two-dimensional force problem. A block of mass m sits on a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, two forces act: its weight mg straight down, and the normal force N perpendicular to the surface. The block can accelerate only along the slope, so choose the axes parallel and perpendicular to it, and resolve the weight into those axes.
hawking.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block does not accelerate, so the forces balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope the only unbalanced force is the component of weight down the slope, mg sinθ. So the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
hawking.5: The mass cancels. The acceleration does not depend on it. Two limits confirm the result. A horizontal surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and zero acceleration. A vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g — free fall, recovered. Draw a free-body diagram before you calculate anything. It is the surest defence against resolving the wrong force, as shown in Figure 1.1.
hawking.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight presses perpendicular only on a horizontal surface. On a slope, only the perpendicular component mg cosθ pushes into the surface, while the parallel component mg sinθ drives the block down. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
hawking.7: Connects The same resolve-into-axes method runs through every problem in this area of study. The banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in exactly this way.
hawking.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once air resistance is ignored. The moment it is released, one force acts on it: its weight mg, straight down. By Newton’s second law its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² downward, constant in magnitude and direction throughout the flight — at the launch, at the top of the path, and everywhere between.
hawking.9: The power of the model lies in this: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and can be analysed apart. No horizontal force acts, so the horizontal velocity stays constant for the whole flight. The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated free fall — identical, when the launch is horizontal, to a body simply dropped from rest. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same instant hit the ground together. Their vertical motions are identical, and the horizontal motion does not touch them.
hawking.10: Key idea Throughout a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² straight down. Horizontal velocity stays constant; vertical velocity changes at 9.8 m s⁻¹ each second. The two motions are independent and share only the time of flight.
hawking.11: One quantity alone links the horizontal and vertical motions: the time. The same elapsed time appears in both sets of equations. That is what lets you solve one motion for the time and feed that time into the other. Apply the constant-acceleration equations separately to each axis, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 horizontally (so horizontally s = uxt):
hawking.12: Fix a sign convention before any substitution, and hold it for the whole problem. Either up is positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or up is negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices give the same physical answer. Mixing them within one problem does not. Resolve a launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal at the outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, then carry these two components through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
hawking.13: Trap A projectile moving upward is still pulled downward. As it rises, the only force on it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole time — even at the very top, where the vertical velocity is momentarily zero. Upward motion does not require an upward force; the constant downward acceleration is simply eating away at the upward velocity. There is no upward force at any stage of the flight.
hawking.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach meet in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh constant, giving the same result as the kinematic method.

munroe.15: You solve a complete projectile problem by resolving the launch velocity into components, working each axis on its own, and using the shared time of flight to connect them. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height, and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
munroe.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level at a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹, aimed 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and we’re ignoring air resistance. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Keep the unrounded intermediate values in the calculator and round only the final answers, to three significant figures.
munroe.17: Formula: resolve the launch velocity into components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
munroe.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
munroe.19: Time of flight. Formula: the ball comes back down to the height it left from, so its vertical displacement over the whole flight is zero. Set s = uyt + 12at² = 0 and solve, which gives t = 2uyg.
munroe.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there’s no acceleration, so distance is just speed times time, R = uxt.
munroe.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so put v = 0 into v² = uy² + 2as and solve for the height, giving H = uy²2g.
munroe.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component never changed, so vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. Stitch them back together for the magnitude, v = √(vx² + vy²), and the direction, θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
munroe.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (i.e. 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
munroe.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed comes out exactly equal to the launch speed, and the landing angle exactly equals the launch angle. That isn’t a coincidence — it’s the symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at the same height.
munroe.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes. Resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to get the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy with Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the one link between the two axes.
munroe.32: Trap The maximum height happens when the vertical velocity is zero, not when the speed is zero. At the top of the path the horizontal velocity ux is still going full strength, so the ball is still moving sideways and its speed there is ux, not zero. Setting the whole speed to zero at the top is a common mistake — the ball stops climbing, but it never stops moving.
munroe.33: Trap Carry the unrounded intermediate values through the whole calculation and round only at the very end. If you substitute a value you’ve already rounded to three significant figures — say, using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — the rounding error piles up and can shift the reported time, range, and height in their final significant figure.
munroe.34: Everything above ignored air resistance, which is an excellent approximation for dense, slow, compact objects over short flights — a thrown rock, say. For light objects, high speeds, or long flights, air resistance stops being negligible, and you can no longer pretend it isn’t there. The VCE study only asks for a qualitative description of what it does.
munroe.35: Air resistance is a force that always opposes the motion — it points in the exact opposite direction to the velocity, at every instant. Since the velocity is tangent to the path and keeps changing direction, the air-resistance force keeps changing direction too, and its size grows as the object goes faster. So it’s not a constant force, which means the tidy constant-acceleration equations stop working.
munroe.36: Here’s what it does, described qualitatively. Air resistance drains energy out of the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range come out smaller than the ideal parabola predicts. The path also goes lopsided: the way down is steeper than the way up, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did going up. The horizontal velocity no longer stays constant — it drops throughout the flight, because air resistance has a horizontal component fighting the horizontal motion. The landing speed is less than the launch speed, and the projectile hits the ground at a steeper angle than it left at.
munroe.37: As a falling object speeds up, the upward air-resistance force grows until it exactly balances the weight. At that moment the net force is zero, so the acceleration is zero, and the object just keeps going at a constant terminal velocity — it stops speeding up. A projectile thrown into a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed instead of accelerating forever. The qualitative picture is summarised in Figure 1.3.
munroe.38: Trap Air resistance does not act only vertically. It opposes the whole velocity, so it has a horizontal component too — which is precisely why the horizontal velocity is no longer constant and why the range shrinks. An answer that keeps the horizontal velocity constant and only “adds drag” to the vertical motion is wrong.
munroe.39: Connects Terminal velocity is just the velocity where the resistive force balances the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning used for a charged particle reaching constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses all through this area of study.

bronowski.0: There is a moment—the instant a body leaves the hand, the bat, the launch ramp—when every contrivance of the launch falls away and one force, and one only, remains to govern it: gravity. The whole of projectile analysis grows from a single seed of an idea: that the horizontal and the vertical motions go their separate ways, independent of one another, and that only the vertical motion is quickened by acceleration.
bronowski.1: Newton’s laws stand unaltered when two or more coplanar forces fall upon a body. The net force is nothing but the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration follows that net force in its own direction, obeying Fnet = ma. When the forces do not lie along one line, we have a method of great power: each force is replaced by its components along two perpendicular axes, the components are gathered separately, and the two axes are then treated as two independent one-dimensional problems.
bronowski.2: A force F pointing at an angle θ to a chosen axis is broken into a component F cosθ lying along that axis and a component F sinθ standing perpendicular to it. The choice of axes is ours to make freely; but a wise choice spares us labour, for we align the axes so that as many forces as possible already lie along them.
bronowski.3: The inclined plane is the oldest and the standard of two-dimensional force problems. A block of mass m rests on a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, two forces act upon it: its weight mg drawn vertically downward, and the normal force N directed perpendicular to the face of the slope. Because the block can move only along the slope, we choose the axes parallel and perpendicular to it, and it is the weight—not the normal force—that we resolve into these axes.
bronowski.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block does not accelerate, so there the forces hold each other in balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope a single unbalanced force remains, the component of weight running down the slope, mg sinθ; and so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
bronowski.5: The acceleration owes nothing to the block’s mass—the mass has vanished from the answer. The two extremes confirm the result, as extremes so often do: a horizontal surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and no acceleration at all, while a vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g, and we have recovered free fall. A free-body diagram, drawn before a single number is touched, is the surest defence against resolving the wrong force, as Figure 1.1 shows.
bronowski.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight presses perpendicular only upon a horizontal surface; on a slope only the perpendicular component mg cosθ bears into the face, while the parallel component mg sinθ drives the block downward. Resolve the weight into the slope axes—never the normal force.
bronowski.7: Connects This same method—resolve into axes—drives every problem in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in just this fashion.
bronowski.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once we agree to set air resistance aside. From the instant of release a single force acts upon it—its weight mg, drawn vertically downward. By Newton’s second law its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward, unchanging in magnitude and in direction through the whole of the flight—at the launch, at the summit of the path, and at every point that lies between.
bronowski.9: The power of the model lies in this: that the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and may be examined each on its own. No horizontal force acts, so the horizontal velocity holds constant through the whole flight. The vertical motion is free fall, uniformly accelerated, the very same motion as a body simply let go from rest if the launch is horizontal. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same instant reach the ground together—for their vertical motions are identical, and the one knows nothing of the other’s horizontal travel.
bronowski.10: Key idea Through a projectile’s whole flight gravity is the only force, so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward. The horizontal velocity holds steady; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ with each passing second. The two motions are independent and share one thing alone: the time of flight.
bronowski.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are bound together by a single quantity: the time. The same elapsed time appears in the horizontal equations and in the vertical, and it is this that lets us solve one motion for the time and then carry that time across into the other. The constant-acceleration equations are applied to each axis apart, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so that horizontally s = uxt):
bronowski.12: A consistent sign convention must be settled before a single substitution is made, and held without wavering for the whole problem. Either up is taken as positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²), or up is taken as negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices yield the same physical answer; only the mixing of them within one problem leads astray. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is resolved at the very outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are then carried through the horizontal and vertical equations each on its own.
bronowski.13: Trap A projectile climbing upward is still pulled downward. As it rises, the only force upon it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward for the whole of the ascent—even at the very summit, where the vertical velocity is for one instant zero. Upward motion demands no upward force; the upward velocity is merely being eaten away by the constant downward acceleration. At no stage of the flight is there any upward force at all.
bronowski.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach meet in the chapter to come: a projectile’s speed at any height may also be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh held constant, and it gives the same result the kinematic method does.
bronowski.15: A complete projectile problem is solved in three movements: resolve the launch velocity into components, analyse each axis on its own, and use the shared time of flight to bind them together. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
bronowski.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is set aside. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so that a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Unrounded intermediate values are kept in the calculator, and only the final answers are rounded, to three significant figures.

bronowski.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
bronowski.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball comes back to its launch height, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, which gives t = 2uyg.
bronowski.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there is no acceleration, so R = uxt.
bronowski.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the summit the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
bronowski.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component is left unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
bronowski.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (that is, 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
bronowski.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed equals the launch speed and the landing angle equals the launch angle—a quiet symmetry, confirming that a projectile launched and landing at the same height ends as it began.
bronowski.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by its axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy by Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single link between the two axes.
bronowski.32: Trap The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity is zero, not when the speed is zero. At the summit of the path the horizontal velocity ux stands undiminished, so the ball is still travelling, and its speed there is ux, not zero. To set the full speed to zero at the top is a common and tempting error.
bronowski.33: Trap Carry the unrounded intermediate values through the whole calculation, and round only at the last. To substitute a value already rounded to three significant figures—using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation, say—lets rounding error gather, and can shift the reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.
bronowski.34: The analysis above sets air resistance aside, which is an excellent approximation for dense, slow, compact bodies over short flights. For light objects, for high speeds, or for long flights, air resistance can no longer be neglected; and here the VCE study asks of us only a qualitative description of its effects.
bronowski.35: Air resistance is a force that forever opposes the motion—it acts at every instant against the direction of the velocity. Because the velocity is tangent to the path and turns ceaselessly, the air-resistance force turns with it through the whole flight, and its magnitude swells with speed. It is, then, no constant force, and the simple constant-acceleration equations hold no longer.
bronowski.36: The consequences may be described in words, qualitatively, as follows. Air resistance draws energy out of the projectile, so that both the maximum height and the range are diminished beside the ideal parabola. The path loses its symmetry and turns asymmetric: the descending part is steeper than the ascending part, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than going up. The horizontal velocity is no longer constant—it wanes throughout the flight, for air resistance has a horizontal component set against the horizontal motion. The landing speed falls short of the launch speed, and the projectile meets the ground at a steeper angle than it left.
bronowski.37: As a falling body gathers speed, the upward air-resistance force grows until it comes to balance the weight. At that point the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the body travels on at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile cast into a long flight tends toward this limiting vertical speed rather than quickening without end. The qualitative picture is gathered in Figure 1.3.
bronowski.38: Trap Air resistance does not act vertically alone. It opposes the whole velocity, so it has a horizontal component too—which is why the horizontal velocity is no longer constant and the range is reduced. A response that holds the horizontal velocity constant while “adding drag” to the vertical motion alone is mistaken.
bronowski.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force comes to balance the weight—the very same force-balance reasoning used for a charged particle reaching a constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses throughout this area of study.

The horizontal velocity is preserved; the vertical velocity changes at 9.8 m s⁻¹ each second. The two motions are independent and share only the time of flight.

The constant-acceleration equations are applied separately to each axis, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so horizontally s = uxt):

Either up is taken as positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or up is taken as negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices preserve the same physical answer; mixing them within one problem does not. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is resolved at the outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are then carried through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.

As it rises, the only force on it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole time — even at the very top, where the vertical velocity is momentarily zero. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.

Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Unrounded intermediate values are retained in the calculator and only final answers are rounded, to three significant figures.

noether.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.

The time of flight is the single link between the two axes.

At the top of the path the horizontal velocity ux remains undiminished — it is the conserved quantity — so the ball is still moving and its speed is ux, not zero. Substituting a value already rounded to three significant figures — for example using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — accumulates rounding error and can shift the reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.

mack.0: The moment a body leaves your hand, the bat, or the launch ramp, the universe simplifies dramatically: gravity is the only force still bothering with it. mack.1: Newton’s laws don’t flinch when two or more coplanar forces pile onto a body. The net force is just the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration dutifully follows Fnet = ma in the direction that net force points. When the forces refuse to line up neatly, you swap each one out for its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components up axis by axis, and — here’s the trick — treat the two axes as two completely independent one-dimensional problems.
mack.2: A force F aimed at an angle θ to some axis you’ve chosen splits into a component F cosθ running along that axis and a component F sinθ standing perpendicular to it. You’re free to point the axes wherever you like — but a clever choice saves you labour: line the axes up so that as many forces as possible are already lying along them, doing the work for you.
mack.3: The inclined plane is the classic two-dimensional force problem, the one you cut your teeth on. Picture a block of mass m sitting on a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, exactly two forces act: its weight mg pulling straight down, and the normal force N pushing perpendicular to the slope’s surface. Since the block can only ever accelerate along the slope, you point the axes parallel and perpendicular to the slope — and then it’s the weight you resolve into those axes.
mack.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block goes nowhere, so the forces there cancel cleanly: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope, the only force left unbalanced is the bit of weight pointing down the incline, mg sinθ, so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
mack.5: Notice the block’s mass has vanished — the acceleration doesn’t care how heavy it is. The two extremes confirm the answer beautifully: a flat surface (θ = 0) hands you N = mg and zero acceleration, while a sheer vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g, which is just free fall reappearing right where it should. mack.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight only presses straight into a horizontal surface; on a slope, just the perpendicular slice mg cosθ pushes into the surface, while the parallel slice mg sinθ goes off and drives the block down the incline. mack.8: A projectile is any body coasting freely under gravity alone, once you agree to ignore air resistance. The instant it’s let go, the only force touching it is its weight mg, pulling straight down. mack.9: The real power of this model is that the horizontal and vertical motions are independent and can be tackled one at a time. There’s no horizontal force at all, so the horizontal velocity simply holds constant from launch to landing. The vertical motion, meanwhile, is plain uniformly accelerated free fall — identical to a body dropped from rest, if the launch happens to be horizontal. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same instant hit the ground together, because their vertical motions are the same and utterly indifferent to whatever the horizontal motion is up to.
