The two extremes confirm it: lay the slope flat (θ = 0) and you get N = mg with no acceleration at all, while stand it vertical (θ = 90°) and you get N = 0 and a = g, plain old free fall again. Sketching a free-body diagram before you touch a single number is your best insurance against resolving the wrong force, as shown in Figure 1.1.
czerski.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight only presses straight into a flat horizontal surface; tilt the surface and just the perpendicular slice, mg cosθ, pushes into it, while the parallel slice mg sinθ sends the block sliding downhill. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
czerski.7: Connects This same resolve-into-axes habit runs through every problem in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter split a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in exactly the same way.
czerski.8: A projectile is anything sailing freely under gravity alone, once you agree to ignore the air pushing back. The instant it’s let go, the only force on it is its weight mg, pointing straight down. So by Newton’s second law its acceleration is g = 9.8 m s⁻² straight down — the same in size and direction at every moment of the flight: at the launch, at the very top of the arc, and at every point along the way.
czerski.9: Here’s where the model earns its keep: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent and you can pick them apart and solve them separately. Nothing pushes the projectile sideways, so its horizontal velocity simply stays the same from start to finish. The vertical motion, meanwhile, is straightforward free fall — identical to what you’d see if you just dropped the thing from rest, when the launch is horizontal. Throw a ball horizontally and drop a second ball from the same height at the same instant, and the two hit the ground together, because their vertical motions are the same and the sideways motion does nothing to change that.
czerski.10: Key idea All through a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration stays a steady 9.8 m s⁻² straight down. The horizontal velocity holds constant; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ every single second. The two motions run independently and share just one thing — the time of flight.
czerski.11: One quantity, and one alone, ties the horizontal and vertical motions together: the time. The very same stretch of elapsed time shows up in both the horizontal and the vertical equations, and that’s exactly what lets you solve one motion for the time and then feed that time into the other. You apply the constant-acceleration equations to each axis on its own, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so horizontally s = uxt):
czerski.12: You must nail down a consistent sign convention before you substitute a single number, and then hold it for the whole problem. Either you call up positive (in which case g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or you call up negative (in which case g = +9.8 m s⁻²). Both choices land on the same physical answer; what gets you into trouble is muddling the two within one problem. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is split right at the start into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and those two components are then carried through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
czerski.13: Trap A projectile heading upward is still being pulled downward. As it climbs, the only force on it is still gravity, so the acceleration stays 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole way — even at the very peak, where the vertical velocity is, for one fleeting instant, zero. Moving up doesn’t need a force pointing up; the upward velocity is simply being whittled away by that constant downward acceleration. At no stage of the flight is there ever an upward force.
czerski.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach come together in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be teased out from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh held constant, which spits out exactly the same result as the kinematic method.
czerski.15: You crack a complete projectile problem the same way every time: split the launch velocity into components, work each axis on its own, and use the shared time of flight to bridge them. The worked example below tracks down the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
czerski.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is ignored. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Unrounded intermediate values are retained in the calculator and only final answers are rounded, to three significant figures.
czerski.17: Formula: resolve the launch velocity into components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
czerski.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
czerski.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball comes back down to its launch height, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, giving t = 2uyg.
czerski.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there’s no acceleration, so R = uxt.
czerski.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
czerski.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component rides through unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
czerski.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (i.e. 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
czerski.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed comes out equal to the launch speed and the landing angle equal to the launch angle — a neat confirmation of the symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at the same height.
czerski.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: split the launch velocity into ux and uy; lean on the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; stitch vx and vy back together with Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single thread linking the two axes.
czerski.32: Trap The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity hits zero, not when the speed hits zero. At the top of the arc the horizontal velocity ux is going strong as ever, so the ball is still very much moving, and its speed is ux, not zero. Setting the whole speed to zero at the top is a classic slip.
czerski.33: Trap Carry unrounded intermediate values right through the calculation and round only at the very end. Plug in a number you’ve already trimmed to three significant figures — say, using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — and the rounding error piles up, nudging your reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.
czerski.34: Everything above quietly ignored air resistance, which is a wonderfully good approximation for dense, slow, compact things flying short distances. But for light objects, high speeds or long flights, the air stops being something you can wave away, and the VCE study asks only for a qualitative description of what it does.
czerski.35: Air resistance is a force that always fights the motion — it points exactly opposite to the velocity at every instant. Because the velocity sits tangent to the path and keeps swinging round to a new direction, the air-resistance force swings round with it throughout the flight, and it grows stronger the faster the object goes. So it’s nothing like a constant force, and the tidy constant-acceleration equations no longer hold.
czerski.36: The upshot, described qualitatively, runs like this. Air resistance drains energy from the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range shrink compared with the ideal parabola. The path turns lopsided: the way down is steeper than the way up, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did climbing. The horizontal velocity no longer holds steady — it bleeds away through the flight, because air resistance has a horizontal component dragging against the horizontal motion. The landing speed comes in under the launch speed, and the projectile hits the ground at a steeper angle than it left.
czerski.37: As a falling object picks up speed, the upward air-resistance force grows and grows until it finally matches the weight. At that moment the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the object just keeps going at a steady terminal velocity. A projectile thrown into a long flight edges toward this limiting vertical speed rather than speeding up without end. The qualitative picture is summarised in Figure 1.3.
czerski.38: Trap Air resistance doesn’t act only up and down. It opposes the whole velocity, so it has a horizontal component too — which is precisely why the horizontal velocity no longer holds constant and the range shrinks. An answer that keeps the horizontal velocity rock-steady while “adding drag” only to the vertical motion has it wrong.
czerski.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force just balances the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning used for a charged particle settling to a constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses throughout this area of study.

veritasium.0: You might think that once a ball leaves your hand the launch somehow keeps pushing it along, that whatever you put into the throw rides with it through the air. It doesn't. The instant a body leaves the hand, the bat or the launch ramp, the launch is over and gravity is the only force acting. And here's the surprise that makes the whole subject simple: the horizontal and vertical motions are completely independent, and only the vertical one accelerates.
veritasium.1: You might expect that adding a second force needs some new rule. It doesn't — Newton’s laws apply unchanged when two or more coplanar forces act on a body. The net force is just the vector sum of the forces, and the acceleration obeys Fnet = ma in the direction of that net force. The trick, when the forces don’t line up, is this: replace each force by its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components separately, and then treat the two axes as two independent one-dimensional problems.
veritasium.2: Here’s the move that does all the work. A force F pointing at an angle θ to a chosen axis splits into a component F cosθ along that axis and a component F sinθ perpendicular to it. You might think the choice of axes is dictated by the page — horizontal and vertical, always. It isn’t; the choice is yours, and a clever choice saves you work: line the axes up so that as many forces as possible already lie along them.
veritasium.3: The inclined plane is the standard two-dimensional force problem, and it’s where most people resolve the wrong force. A block of mass m rests on a slope inclined at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, only two forces act: its weight mg pointing straight down, and the normal force N pointing perpendicular to the slope surface. Now, your instinct is probably to keep the axes horizontal and vertical — but the block can only accelerate along the slope, so you choose axes parallel and perpendicular to the slope, and it’s the weight that gets resolved into them.
veritasium.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block doesn’t accelerate, so those forces balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope the only unbalanced force is the component of weight running down the slope, mg sinθ, so the acceleration of a block on a frictionless slope is:
veritasium.5: Notice what dropped out: the acceleration doesn’t depend on the block’s mass at all. And the two extremes confirm it. A horizontal surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and zero acceleration, exactly as you’d expect; a vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g, which is just free fall again. The surest way to avoid resolving the wrong force is to draw a free-body diagram before you calculate anything, as shown in Figure 1.1.
veritasium.6: Trap You almost certainly want to write the normal force on an inclined plane as mg. It isn’t — it’s N = mg cosθ. The full weight presses perpendicular only on a horizontal surface; on a slope, only the perpendicular component mg cosθ pushes into the surface, while the parallel component mg sinθ drives the block down the slope. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
veritasium.7: Connects This same resolve-into-axes move runs through every problem in the area of study. The banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in exactly this way — same idea, different setting.
veritasium.8: So what is a projectile? Just any body moving freely under gravity alone, once you ignore air resistance. You might imagine that a fast-launched ball carries some forward force with it — but the moment it’s released, the only force acting is its weight mg, pointing straight down. By Newton’s second law its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² straight down, constant in both magnitude and direction for the whole flight — at the launch, at the very top of the path, and at every point in between.
veritasium.9: Here’s the idea that makes projectiles tractable: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent and can be handled completely separately. There’s no horizontal force, so the horizontal velocity never changes throughout the flight. The vertical motion is uniformly accelerated free fall — identical, if the launch is horizontal, to simply dropping the body from rest. This sounds wrong, but it’s true: a ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same instant hit the ground together, because their vertical motions are identical and the horizontal motion changes nothing.
veritasium.10: Key idea Throughout a projectile’s flight the only force is gravity, so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² straight down. Horizontal velocity stays constant; vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ every second. The two motions are independent and share just one thing — the time of flight.
veritasium.11: You might wonder how two independent motions ever talk to each other. They’re linked by exactly one quantity: the time. The same elapsed time shows up in both the horizontal and the vertical equations, and that’s precisely what lets you solve one motion for the time and feed that time into the other. You apply the constant-acceleration equations separately to each axis, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal direction (so horizontally s = uxt):
veritasium.12: Before you substitute a single number, fix a sign convention and hold it for the whole problem — this is where careless answers come from. Either up is positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or up is negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). Both choices give the same physical answer; mixing them inside one problem does not. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is resolved right at the start into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are then carried through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
veritasium.13: Trap A projectile moving upward feels like it must have an upward force — it’s going up, after all. It doesn’t. As it rises, the only force on it is still gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the entire time — even at the very top, where the vertical velocity is momentarily zero. Upward motion doesn’t require an upward force; the upward velocity is just being eaten away by the constant downward acceleration. There is no upward force at any stage of the flight.
veritasium.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach meet in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh constant, and it gives the same answer as the kinematic method — two roads, one destination.
veritasium.15: Put it all together and a complete projectile problem is just three steps: resolve the launch velocity into components, analyse each axis on its own, and use the shared time of flight to connect them. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
veritasium.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is ignored. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Unrounded intermediate values are kept in the calculator and only final answers are rounded, to three significant figures.
veritasium.17: Formula: resolve the launch velocity into components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
veritasium.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
veritasium.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball returns to its launch height, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, giving t = 2uyg.
veritasium.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there is no acceleration, so R = uxt.
veritasium.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
veritasium.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component is unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
veritasium.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (i.e. 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
veritasium.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. Look at that — the landing speed equals the launch speed and the landing angle equals the launch angle, exactly, confirming the symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at the same height.
veritasium.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; use the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; recombine vx and vy by Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single link between the two axes.
veritasium.32: Trap You’d naturally say the projectile stops at the top — the maximum height is reached when its speed is zero. It isn’t. The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity is zero. At the top of the path the horizontal velocity ux is completely undiminished, so the ball is still moving, and its speed there is ux, not zero. Setting the full speed to zero at the top is a common error.
veritasium.33: Trap It seems harmless to round as you go — it isn’t. Carry unrounded intermediate values right through the calculation and round only at the very end. Substituting a value already rounded to three significant figures — for example using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — piles up rounding error and can shift the reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.
veritasium.34: Everything above quietly ignored air resistance. You might assume that’s a cop-out — but for dense, slow, compact objects over short flights it’s an excellent approximation, close enough to be effectively exact. It’s only for light objects, high speeds or long flights that air resistance stops being negligible, and even then the VCE study asks of you just one thing: a qualitative description of its effects.
veritasium.35: Air resistance is a force that always opposes the motion — it points exactly opposite to the velocity at every instant. Here’s why that makes it awkward: the velocity is tangential to the path and keeps changing direction, so the air-resistance force keeps swinging direction too, and its magnitude climbs with speed. It is therefore not a constant force, and the simple constant-acceleration equations no longer apply.
veritasium.36: So what actually changes? Air resistance drains energy from the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range shrink compared with the ideal parabola. The path turns asymmetric: the way down is steeper than the way up, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did going up. The horizontal velocity is no longer constant — you might still expect it to be, but it isn’t; it drops throughout the flight, because air resistance has a horizontal component fighting the horizontal motion. The landing speed comes in below the launch speed, and the projectile hits the ground at a steeper angle than it left.
veritasium.37: You might think a falling object just keeps speeding up forever. It doesn’t. As it falls faster, the upward air-resistance force grows until it exactly balances the weight. At that point the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the object keeps going at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile launched into a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed rather than accelerating without end. The qualitative picture is summarised in Figure 1.3.
veritasium.38: Trap It’s tempting to picture air resistance as acting only vertically. It doesn’t — it opposes the whole velocity, so it has a horizontal component too, which is exactly why the horizontal velocity is no longer constant and the range is reduced. An answer that keeps the horizontal velocity constant while “adding drag” only to the vertical motion is wrong.
veritasium.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force balances the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning used for a charged particle reaching constant speed in a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses throughout this area of study.

attenborough.0: Once a body leaves the hand, the bat, or the launch ramp, it is alone with gravity — that single, patient force, and no other. And from this one quiet fact the whole of projectile motion unfolds: the horizontal and the vertical motions go their separate ways, independent of one another, and only the vertical motion ever feels the pull.
attenborough.1: Newton’s laws hold, unchanged, when two or more coplanar forces act together upon a body. The net force is the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration follows faithfully along its line, obeying Fnet = ma. When the forces do not lie along a single line, we do something rather elegant: we replace each force by its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components separately, and then treat each axis as its own independent one-dimensional world.
attenborough.2: A force F leaning at an angle θ to a chosen axis is resolved into a component F cosθ lying along that axis and a component F sinθ standing perpendicular to it. We are free to point our axes wherever we please — but the wise choice spares us labour, aligning the axes so that as many forces as possible already lie obediently along them.
attenborough.3: The inclined plane is the classic two-dimensional puzzle of forces. Picture a block of mass m resting upon a slope that tilts at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, just two forces lay claim to it: its weight mg, pulling straight down toward the centre of the Earth, and the normal force N, pressing outward, perpendicular to the surface of the slope. Because the block can only ever slide along the slope, we point our axes parallel and perpendicular to it — and it is the weight, this time, that we resolve into those axes.
attenborough.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block does not accelerate; here the forces hold each other in perfect balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope, only one force goes unanswered — the component of weight tugging the block downhill, mg sinθ — and so the acceleration of a block upon a frictionless slope is:
attenborough.5: Remarkably, the acceleration cares nothing for the block’s mass. And the two extremes confirm it beautifully: a flat surface (θ = 0) gives N = mg and no acceleration at all, while a sheer vertical drop (θ = 90°) gives N = 0 and a = g — and we are returned, once more, to free fall. A free-body diagram, drawn before a single calculation is begun, remains the surest guard against resolving the wrong force, as we see in Figure 1.1.
attenborough.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight presses perpendicular only upon a horizontal surface; on a slope, just the perpendicular component mg cosθ bears down into the surface, while the parallel component mg sinθ sends the block sliding away down the hill. Resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
attenborough.7: Connects This same act of resolving into axes drives every problem across this area of study: the banked-track and string problems in the circular-motion chapter resolve a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in precisely this way.
attenborough.8: A projectile is any body moving freely under gravity alone, once we agree to ignore the air. The very instant it is released, only one force remains to act upon it — its weight mg, pulling straight down. And so, by Newton’s second law, its acceleration is g = 9.8 m s⁻² directed vertically downward, constant in both magnitude and direction for the whole of its journey — at the launch, at the highest point of its arc, and at every instant in between.
attenborough.9: Here lies the quiet power of the model: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, and may be watched quite separately. There is no horizontal force, and so the horizontal velocity holds steady throughout the entire flight. The vertical motion, meanwhile, is uniformly accelerated free fall — identical, if the launch is horizontal, to a body simply let go from rest. A ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same moment will reach the ground together, hand in hand, for their vertical motions are one and the same, untroubled by the horizontal.

The horizontal velocity stays constant; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ each passing second. The two motions are independent, sharing one thing alone — the time of flight.
attenborough.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are bound together by a single thread: the time. The very same elapsed time appears in both the horizontal and the vertical equations — and this is what lets us solve one motion for the time, then carry that time across into the other. The constant-acceleration equations are applied to each axis on its own, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal (so that horizontally s = uxt):

A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal is resolved, right at the outset, into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are then carried independently through the horizontal and vertical equations.

As it rises, the only force upon it remains gravity, so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the entire time — even at the very summit, where, for one fleeting instant, the vertical velocity is zero. Upward motion demands no upward force; the rising velocity is merely being whittled away by that steady downward pull. At no moment of the flight is there any upward force at all.

But for light objects, for high speeds, or for long journeys through the air, resistance can no longer be ignored, and the VCE study asks only for a qualitative description of what it does.
attenborough.35: Air resistance is a force that forever opposes the motion — at every instant it points in the direction exactly opposite to the velocity. And because the velocity runs tangent to the path, forever turning, the air-resistance force turns with it throughout the flight, growing in magnitude as the speed rises. It is, therefore, no constant force, and the simple constant-acceleration equations can serve us no longer.
attenborough.36: The consequences may be described qualitatively, like this. Air resistance steals energy from the projectile, and so both the maximum height and the range are reduced below those of the ideal parabola. The path turns asymmetric: the descent is steeper than the climb, and the projectile covers less horizontal ground coming down than it did going up. The horizontal velocity no longer holds steady — it dwindles throughout the flight, for air resistance has a horizontal component set against the horizontal motion. The landing speed falls short of the launch speed, and the projectile meets the ground at a steeper angle than the one at which it left.
attenborough.37: As a falling object gathers speed, the upward force of air resistance swells until, at last, it balances the weight. At that moment the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the object falls on at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile cast into a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed, rather than quickening forever without end. The qualitative picture is gathered together in Figure 1.3.
attenborough.38: Trap Air resistance does not act in the vertical alone. It opposes the whole velocity, and so it carries a horizontal component too — which is precisely why the horizontal velocity no longer holds constant and the range is cut short. A response that keeps the horizontal velocity steady while “adding drag” only to the vertical motion is mistaken.
attenborough.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force comes into balance with the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning that governs a charged particle settling to a constant speed in a viscous medium, and the energy-dissipation analyses found throughout this area of study.

greekepic.0: Once the body parts from the hand, from the bat, or from the launching ramp, gravity alone holds dominion over it, and no other force lays claim. The whole art of the projectile flows from a single decree: that the horizontal motion and the vertical motion go their separate ways, untouched by one another, and that the vertical alone is quickened by acceleration.
greekepic.1: The laws of Newton keep their faith unbroken when two forces, or many, act together upon a body in one plane. The net force is the vector-gathering of them all, and the acceleration follows that net force in its own direction, obedient to Fnet = ma. Yet when the forces lie not along one line, each is broken into its components upon two axes set at right angles; the components are gathered each to its own kind; and the two axes are thereafter ruled as two separate kingdoms, each a single problem in one dimension.
greekepic.2: A force F aimed at an angle θ to a chosen axis is sundered into a component F cosθ running along that axis and a component F sinθ standing perpendicular to it. The choosing of axes is left free to the watcher; yet a wise choosing spares labour, for the axes are set so that as many forces as may be already lie along them.
greekepic.3: The inclined plane is the appointed two-dimensional trial of forces. A block of mass m rests upon a slope reared at angle θ to the level earth. Two forces lay hold of it, if the slope be without friction: its weight mg drawn straight downward toward the ground, and the normal force N driven perpendicular to the face of the slope. Because the block may be quickened only along the slope, the axes are set parallel and perpendicular to that slope, and it is the weight that is broken into these axes.
greekepic.4: Perpendicular to the slope the block is not quickened at all, and there the forces hold one another in balance: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope a single force goes unanswered, the component of weight that drives down the slope, mg sinθ; and so the acceleration of a block upon a frictionless slope is decreed:
greekepic.5: The acceleration cares nothing for the mass of the block. The two limits bear witness to the truth of it: a level surface (θ = 0) yields N = mg and no acceleration at all, while a sheer plunge (θ = 90°) yields N = 0 and a = g, restoring the free fall of old. A free-body diagram, drawn before any reckoning is begun, is the surest shield against breaking the wrong force into its parts, as is shown in Figure 1.1.
greekepic.6: Trap Mark well: the normal force upon an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The whole weight presses perpendicular only upon a level surface; upon a slope, only the perpendicular component mg cosθ bears down into the face, while the parallel component mg sinθ drives the block down the slope. Break the weight into the slope's own axes — never the normal force.
greekepic.7: Connects This selfsame method of breaking forces into axes drives every trial in this area of study: the banked-track and string problems of the circular-motion chapter sunder a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in precisely this manner.
greekepic.8: A projectile is any body journeying freely under gravity alone, once the resistance of the air is set aside. In the very instant of its release, the one force laying hold of it is its weight mg, drawn straight downward toward the earth. By the second law of Newton its acceleration is therefore g = 9.8 m s⁻² directed straight downward, unchanging in magnitude and in direction through the whole of its flight — at the launch, at the summit of its path, and at every point that lies between.
greekepic.9: The power of this model is that the horizontal motion and the vertical motion are independent, and may be reckoned each apart from the other. There is no horizontal force, and so the horizontal velocity holds constant through the whole flight. The vertical motion is free fall, uniformly quickened, the very same as the motion of a body let drop from rest, if the launch be horizontal. A ball cast sideways and a ball let fall from the same height in the same instant strike the ground as one, for their vertical motions are identical and untouched by the horizontal.
greekepic.10: Key idea Through the whole of a projectile's flight the one force is gravity, and so the acceleration is a constant 9.8 m s⁻² directed straight downward. The horizontal velocity holds constant; the vertical velocity is altered by 9.8 m s⁻¹ with every passing second. The two motions go their independent ways, and share one thing alone — the time of flight.
greekepic.11: The horizontal motion and the vertical are bound together by one quantity, and one alone: the time. The same elapsed time appears in the horizontal equations and in the vertical, and it is this that suffers one motion to be solved for the time, and that time then carried into the other. The equations of constant acceleration are laid upon each axis apart, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 in the horizontal (so that horizontally s = uxt):
greekepic.12: A steadfast sign convention must be fixed before any value is set down, and kept through the whole of the problem. Either up is taken as positive (and then g = −9.8 m s⁻²), or up is taken as negative (and then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). The two choices yield one and the same physical answer; the mingling of them within one problem does not. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the level is broken at the very outset into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and these two components are thereafter borne through the horizontal and vertical equations, each in its own course.
greekepic.13: Trap A projectile rising is yet drawn downward still. As it climbs, the one force upon it remains gravity, and so the acceleration is 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole while — even at the very summit, where the vertical velocity is for an instant nothing at all. Upward motion demands no upward force; the upward velocity is merely being lessened by the unchanging downward acceleration. There is no upward force at any stage of the flight.
greekepic.14: Connects The independence of components and the way of work and energy meet in the chapter to come: a projectile's speed at any height may likewise be found from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh held constant, yielding the same result as the kinematic way.
greekepic.15: A whole projectile problem is brought to its end by breaking the launch velocity into components, by reckoning each axis apart, and by using the shared time of flight to bind them together. The worked example below finds the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
greekepic.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is ignored. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Unrounded intermediate values are retained in the calculator and only final answers are rounded, to three significant figures.
greekepic.17: Formula: break the launch velocity into its components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
greekepic.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
greekepic.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball returns to its launch height, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, giving t = 2uyg.
greekepic.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there is no acceleration, so R = uxt.
greekepic.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity is zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
greekepic.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component is unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
greekepic.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (i.e. 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
greekepic.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed is equal to the launch speed and the landing angle equal to the launch angle, bearing witness to the symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at one same height.
greekepic.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by its axes: break the launch velocity into ux and uy; wield the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; wield R = uxt for the range; gather vx and vy together again by Pythagoras and by trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the single bond between the two axes.
greekepic.32: Trap The maximum height is reached when the vertical velocity is zero, not when the speed is zero. At the summit of the path the horizontal velocity ux goes undiminished, and so the ball is moving yet, and its speed is ux, not nothing. To set the whole speed to zero at the top is an error oft committed.
greekepic.33: Trap Bear the unrounded intermediate values through the whole reckoning, and round the final answer alone. To set down a value already rounded to three significant figures — for instance to use uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — heaps up rounding error, and may shift the reported time, range and height in the final significant figure.
greekepic.34: The analysis above sets aside the resistance of the air, which is a noble approximation for dense, slow, compact bodies over short flights. For light bodies, for great speeds, or for long flights, air resistance can no longer be set aside, and the VCE study asks only a qualitative telling of its effects.
greekepic.35: Air resistance is a force that forever opposes the motion — it acts in the direction set against the velocity at every instant. Because the velocity lies tangent to the path and turns continually in its direction, so too the air-resistance force turns its direction through the whole flight, and its magnitude swells with the speed. It is therefore no constant force, and the simple equations of constant acceleration hold sway no longer.
greekepic.36: The consequences are told qualitatively as follows. Air resistance draws energy out of the projectile, and so both the maximum height and the range are lessened against the ideal parabola. The path is rendered asymmetric: the descending part of the path is steeper than the ascending, and so the projectile travels less horizontal distance coming down than going up. The horizontal velocity holds constant no longer — it dwindles through the whole flight, for air resistance bears a horizontal component set against the horizontal motion. The landing speed is less than the launch speed, and the projectile strikes the earth at a steeper angle than that at which it was launched.
greekepic.37: As a falling body gathers speed, the upward air-resistance force grows until it balances the weight. At that point the net force is nothing, the acceleration is nothing, and the body journeys onward at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile cast into a long flight tends toward this limiting vertical speed, rather than quickening without end. The qualitative picture is gathered in Figure 1.3.
greekepic.38: Trap Air resistance does not act in the vertical alone. It opposes the whole velocity, and so it bears a horizontal component too — which is why the horizontal velocity holds constant no longer and the range is lessened. A response that keeps the horizontal velocity constant while “adding drag” to the vertical motion alone is in error.
greekepic.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force balances the weight — the same force-balance reasoning wielded for a charged particle reaching constant speed within a viscous medium, and for the energy-dissipation analyses throughout this area of study.

cnpop.0: 注意看，球一旦离开你的手、离开球棒、或者离开发射坡道，整个世界就只剩重力一个人在跟它较劲了。抛体分析这么大一摊东西，归根结底就靠一句话：水平方向和竖直方向各玩各的，互不干扰，而且只有竖直方向才会加速。
cnpop.1: 好消息是，就算两个甚至更多共面的力一起压在一个物体身上，牛顿定律照样原封不动地好用。合力就是这些力的矢量和，加速度则乖乖沿着合力的方向服从 Fnet = ma。要是这些力不在同一条线上也别慌：把每个力拆成沿两条互相垂直的坐标轴的分量，分量各自相加，然后这两条轴就当成两个互不相干的一维问题分头解决——是不是一下子清爽了？
cnpop.2: 一个跟你选定坐标轴成 θ 角的力 F，可以拆成沿轴的分量 F cosθ 和垂直于轴的分量 F sinθ。坐标轴你爱怎么选都行，但聪明的选法能帮你省一大堆活：把轴摆得让尽可能多的力本来就躺在轴上，事半功倍。
cnpop.3: 斜面就是二维力问题里的祖师爷、压轴常客。一个质量为 m 的木块停在一个跟水平面成 θ 角的斜坡上。如果斜面没摩擦，它身上就只有两个力：竖直向下的重力 mg，以及垂直于斜面表面的法向力 N。由于木块只能沿着斜面加速，我们就把坐标轴选成平行和垂直于斜面，而真正要被拆分的，是那个重力。
cnpop.4: 垂直于斜面的方向上木块不加速，所以这边的力是平衡的：N = mg cosθ。而沿斜面方向，唯一没被抵消的力就是重力沿坡下的分量 mg sinθ，于是无摩擦斜面上木块的加速度就是：
cnpop.5: 加速度跟木块的质量半毛钱关系都没有。两个极端情形帮你验货：水平面（θ = 0）给出 N = mg 加速度为零，而竖直下落（θ = 90°）给出 N = 0、a = g，自由落体华丽回归。开算之前先老老实实画一张受力分析图，这是防止你拆错力的最稳保命符，参见 Figure 1.1。
cnpop.6: Trap 斜面上的法向力是 N = mg cosθ，不是 mg，别想当然。只有在水平面上，整个重力才会全压在表面上；一上斜坡，就只有垂直分量 mg cosθ 往表面里挤，而平行分量 mg sinθ 负责把木块往坡下推。记牢一句话：要拆的是重力，往斜面的两条轴上拆——千万别去拆法向力。
cnpop.7: Connects 这套“拆到轴上”的招式，是本单元每道题的通用打法：圆周运动那一章里的倾斜赛道和绳子问题，把法向力或张力拆成水平和竖直分量，跟这里的玩法一模一样，换汤不换药。
cnpop.8: 抛体就是任何只在重力作用下自由运动的物体——前提是把空气阻力忽略掉。物体一被释放，它身上唯一的力就是竖直向下的重力 mg。按牛顿第二定律，它的加速度因此就是竖直向下的 g = 9.8 m s⁻²，而且大小和方向在整个飞行过程中纹丝不动——发射那一刻、飞到最高点、以及中间的每一个点，全都一样。
cnpop.9: 这个模型的杀手锏在于：水平运动和竖直运动是互相独立的，可以分开来各算各的。水平方向没有力，所以整个飞行过程水平速度恒定不变。竖直方向则是匀加速自由落体——要是水平发射，它跟一个原地静止往下扔的物体的运动一模一样。一个水平抛出的球和一个从同样高度同一瞬间松手下落的球，会同时落地，因为它俩的竖直运动完全相同，丝毫不受水平运动的影响。
cnpop.10: Key idea 抛体飞行全程唯一的力就是重力，所以加速度是恒定的 9.8 m s⁻² 竖直向下。水平速度恒定不变；竖直速度每秒变化 9.8 m s⁻¹。两种运动彼此独立，唯一共享的就是飞行时间。
cnpop.11: 水平运动和竖直运动之间，只靠一个量牵线搭桥：时间。同一段流逝的时间会同时出现在水平方程和竖直方程里，正因如此，你才能先用一种运动把时间解出来，再把这个时间喂给另一种运动。匀加速方程对每条轴分别套用，竖直方程里加速度 a = g，水平方向 a = 0（所以水平方向就是 s = uxt）：
cnpop.12: 动手代数之前，必须先把符号约定钉死，并且整道题从头到尾死守不变。要么把向上取为正（那 g = −9.8 m s⁻²），要么把向上取为负（那 g = +9.8 m s⁻²）。两种选法给出的物理答案完全一样；但你要是在一道题里把它俩混着用，那就翻车了。一个以 θ 角斜向上、速率为 v 的发射速度，一开始就要拆成 ux = v cosθ 和 uy = v sinθ，然后这两个分量各自独立地代进水平和竖直方程里走完全程。
cnpop.13: Trap 一个正在往上飞的抛体，照样被往下拽，别被它的上升骗了。它往上窜的时候，身上唯一的力还是重力，所以加速度全程都是 9.8 m s⁻² 向下——哪怕在最高点、竖直速度刚好瞬间为零的那一刻也是如此。往上的运动压根不需要往上的力；向上的速度只是在被那个恒定的向下加速度一点点削掉而已。整个飞行过程中，没有任何一个向上的力，一个都没有。
cnpop.14: Connects 分量独立和功能关系会在下一章正面相遇：抛体在任意高度的速率也能用机械能守恒求出来，½mv² + mgh 是常数，给出的结果跟运动学方法分毫不差。
cnpop.15: 一道完整的抛体题，解法套路就三步：把发射速度拆成分量，每条轴分头分析，再用共享的飞行时间把它们串起来。下面这道例题会把斜向发射的飞行时间、射程、最大高度和落地速度全部求出来。抛物线轨迹及其速度分量见 Figure 1.2。
cnpop.16: 小梅从地面踢出一个球，速率 25.0 m s⁻¹，方向与水平面成 35.0° 角斜向上。地面是水平的，空气阻力忽略不计。取 g = 9.8 m s⁻²，向上为正，所以竖直方程里 a = −9.8 m s⁻²。中间过程的未舍入数值全留在计算器里，只对最终答案取舍入，保留三位有效数字。
cnpop.17: 公式：把发射速度拆成分量，ux = v cosθ 和 uy = v sinθ。
cnpop.18: 代入：ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹；uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹。
cnpop.19: 飞行时间。公式：竖直方向上球回到发射高度，所以竖直位移为零，s = uyt + 12at² = 0，给出 t = 2uyg。
cnpop.22: 射程。公式：水平方向没有加速度，所以 R = uxt。
cnpop.25: 最大高度。公式：在最高点竖直速度为零，所以用 v² = uy² + 2as 配上 v = 0，得到 H = uy²2g。
cnpop.28: 落地时的末速度。公式：水平分量原封不动，vx = ux；竖直分量是 vy = uy + at。大小是 v = √(vx² + vy²)，方向是水平线以下 θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx)。
cnpop.29: 代入：vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹；vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹（也就是 14.3 m s⁻¹ 向下）；v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²)；θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788)。
cnpop.30: 答案：v = 25.0 m s⁻¹，方向在水平线以下 35.0°。落地速率跟发射速率一模一样，落地角度也跟发射角度一模一样，稳稳印证了一个从同一高度发射又落回同一高度的抛体那份完美对称。
cnpop.31: Key idea 解抛体题就认准“分轴”这套路：把发射速度拆成 ux 和 uy；用竖直方程（带 a = g）求时间和高度；用 R = uxt 求射程；最后靠勾股定理和三角函数把 vx 和 vy 重新拼回去得到末速度。飞行时间，就是连通两条轴的唯一桥梁。
cnpop.32: Trap 最大高度是在竖直速度为零时达到的，不是速率为零时，这俩可别搞混。在轨迹最高点，水平速度 ux 一点没减，所以球还在动呢，它的速率是 ux，绝不是零。把最高点的整个速率当成零，是个超高频翻车点。
cnpop.33: Trap 中间数值不要提前舍入，带着完整精度算到最后，只对终答取舍入。要是你把已经取到三位有效数字的值代回去——比如在时间方程里用 uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹——舍入误差就会一路滚雪球，足以把报告出来的时间、射程和高度在最后一位有效数字上整偏了。
cnpop.34: 上面这套分析把空气阻力忽略掉了——对于密度大、速度慢、又紧实小巧、飞行距离又短的物体来说，这是个相当顶的近似。可一旦换成轻飘飘的物体、高速、或者长距离飞行，空气阻力就不能再当空气了，而 VCE 课程对它只要求一个定性的描述。
cnpop.35: 空气阻力是一个永远跟运动对着干的力——它在每一瞬间都指向速度的反方向。由于速度沿着轨迹切线、又一直在改变方向，空气阻力这个力也就跟着在整个飞行过程中不停改方向，而且它的大小还随速度增大而增大。所以它压根不是个恒力，那套省心的匀加速方程在这儿也就失效了。
cnpop.36: 它带来的后果，定性地说是这样的。空气阻力会从抛体身上抽走能量，所以跟理想抛物线相比，最大高度和射程都会缩水。轨迹会变得不对称：下降段比上升段更陡，所以抛体下来时走过的水平距离比上去时要短。水平速度也不再恒定——它在整个飞行过程中一路减小，因为空气阻力有一个水平分量在跟水平运动唱反调。落地速率比发射速率小，而且抛体砸地的角度比发射时更陡。
cnpop.37: 一个下落的物体越掉越快，那个向上的空气阻力也越长越大，直到把重力顶平。到那一刻，合力为零，加速度为零，物体就以一个恒定的终极速度（terminal velocity）继续往下走。一个被甩进长途飞行的抛体，会朝着这个竖直速度的上限收敛，而不是一直加速到天荒地老。这幅定性图景总结在 Figure 1.3 里。
cnpop.38: Trap 空气阻力可不是只往竖直方向使劲。它对着干的是整个速度，所以它也有一个水平分量——这正是水平速度不再恒定、射程被砍掉的原因。要是有人把水平速度按住不动、只往竖直运动上“加点阻力”，那答案就是错的。
cnpop.39: Connects 终极速度就是阻力把重力顶平时的那个速度——这套“力平衡”的推理，跟一个带电粒子在黏滞介质中达到恒定速率用的是同一套逻辑，也跟贯穿本单元的能量耗散分析一脉相承。

ai.0: The moment a body leaves your hand, the bat or the launch ramp, gravity has it all to itself — nothing else is pushing or pulling. And the entire art of analysing projectiles flows from a single, beautiful idea: the horizontal and vertical motions go their own separate ways, and only the vertical one ever feels the pull.
ai.1: Newton’s laws don’t flinch when two or more coplanar forces gang up on a body. The net force is just the vector sum of them all, and the acceleration follows that net force’s direction obediently through Fnet = ma. When the forces don’t line up, here’s the trick: swap each one for its components along two perpendicular axes, add the components up axis by axis, and you’ve turned one awkward problem into two tidy one-dimensional ones.
ai.2: A force F pointing at an angle θ to an axis splits cleanly into a piece F cosθ running along that axis and a piece F sinθ at right angles to it. You’re free to point your axes wherever you like — but choose wisely and you save yourself work: line them up so that as many forces as possible already lie flat along them.
ai.3: The inclined plane is the classic two-dimensional force puzzle, and it’s worth getting comfortable with. Picture a block of mass m sitting on a slope tilted at angle θ to the horizontal. If the slope is frictionless, just two forces act: its weight mg pulling straight down, and the normal force N pushing out perpendicular to the slope surface. Since the block can only slide along the slope, you point your axes parallel and perpendicular to that slope — and it’s the weight you resolve into them, not the normal force.
ai.4: Perpendicular to the slope nothing accelerates, so the forces there cancel out: N = mg cosθ. Parallel to the slope, the only force left unbalanced is the slice of weight pointing down the incline, mg sinθ — and that’s what drives the block. So the acceleration of a block sliding down a frictionless slope is simply:
ai.5: Notice the mass has vanished — the acceleration doesn’t care how heavy the block is. The two extremes confirm we’re on the right track: lay the slope flat (θ = 0) and you get N = mg with no acceleration at all, while tip it to a sheer drop (θ = 90°) and you get N = 0 with a = g, which is just free fall reappearing. Sketching a free-body diagram before you touch any numbers is your surest defence against resolving the wrong force, as shown in Figure 1.1.
ai.6: Trap The normal force on an inclined plane is N = mg cosθ, not mg. The full weight only presses straight into a horizontal surface; on a slope, just the perpendicular slice mg cosθ pushes into the surface, while the parallel slice mg sinθ sends the block sliding down. Always resolve the weight into the slope axes — never the normal force.
ai.7: Connects This same resolve-into-axes move powers every problem in this area of study. The banked-track and string problems over in the circular-motion chapter split a normal force or a tension into horizontal and vertical components in exactly the same spirit.
ai.8: A projectile is any body coasting freely under gravity alone, once we agree to ignore air resistance. From the instant it’s let go, the one and only force on it is its weight mg, pointing straight down. So by Newton’s second law its acceleration is g = 9.8 m s⁻² straight down — rock-steady in both size and direction for the whole flight: at the launch, at the very peak of the arc, and at every instant in between.
ai.9: Here’s where the model earns its keep: the horizontal and vertical motions are independent, so you can pick them apart and handle each on its own. Nothing pushes horizontally, so the horizontal velocity simply stays constant the whole way. The vertical motion, meanwhile, is plain uniformly-accelerated free fall — for a horizontal launch it’s identical to just dropping the thing from rest. That’s why a ball thrown horizontally and a ball dropped from the same height at the same moment hit the ground together: their vertical motions are one and the same, completely untouched by whatever the horizontal motion is doing.
ai.10: Key idea Throughout a projectile’s flight the only force in play is gravity, so the acceleration holds at a constant 9.8 m s⁻² straight down. The horizontal velocity stays put; the vertical velocity changes by 9.8 m s⁻¹ every second. The two motions run independently and share just one thing — the time of flight.
ai.11: The horizontal and vertical motions are tied together by a single quantity: the time. That same elapsed time turns up in both the horizontal and the vertical equations, which is exactly what lets you solve one motion for the time and then hand that time over to the other. You apply the constant-acceleration equations to each axis on its own, with acceleration a = g in the vertical equations and a = 0 horizontally (so horizontally s = uxt):
ai.12: Lock in a consistent sign convention before you substitute a single number, and hold it for the entire problem. Either call up positive (then g = −9.8 m s⁻²) or call up negative (then g = +9.8 m s⁻²). Both choices land on the same physical answer — what you mustn’t do is mix them within one problem. A launch velocity v at angle θ above the horizontal gets resolved right at the start into ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ, and those two components are then carried through the horizontal and vertical equations independently.
ai.13: Trap A projectile heading upward is still being pulled down. As it climbs, the only force on it is gravity, so the acceleration stays 9.8 m s⁻² downward the whole time — yes, even at the very top, where the vertical velocity is fleetingly zero. Upward motion simply doesn’t need an upward force; that upward velocity is just being eaten away by the steady downward acceleration. There is no upward force at any moment of the flight.
ai.14: Connects The independence of components and the work–energy approach shake hands in the next chapter: a projectile’s speed at any height can also be dug out from conservation of mechanical energy, ½mv² + mgh constant, and it hands you the very same result as the kinematic method.
ai.15: You crack a complete projectile problem in three moves: resolve the launch velocity into components, work each axis on its own, then use the shared time of flight to stitch them back together. The worked example below tracks down the time of flight, the range, the maximum height and the final velocity for a launch at an angle. The parabolic path and its velocity components are shown in Figure 1.2.
ai.16: Mei kicks a ball from ground level with a speed of 25.0 m s⁻¹ at an angle of 35.0° above the horizontal. The ground is level and air resistance is ignored. Take g = 9.8 m s⁻² and up as positive, so a = −9.8 m s⁻² in the vertical equations. Keep the unrounded intermediate values sitting in the calculator and round only the final answers, to three significant figures.
ai.17: Formula: resolve the launch velocity into its components, ux = v cosθ and uy = v sinθ.
ai.18: Substitution: ux = 25.0 × cos35.0° = 20.5 m s⁻¹; uy = 25.0 × sin35.0° = 14.3 m s⁻¹.
ai.19: Time of flight. Formula: vertically the ball comes back to the height it left, so its vertical displacement is zero, s = uyt + 12at² = 0, which hands you t = 2uyg.
ai.22: Range. Formula: horizontally there’s no acceleration to worry about, so R = uxt.
ai.25: Maximum height. Formula: at the top the vertical velocity drops to zero, so v² = uy² + 2as with v = 0 gives H = uy²2g.
ai.28: Final velocity at landing. Formula: the horizontal component sails through unchanged, vx = ux; the vertical component is vy = uy + at. The magnitude is v = √(vx² + vy²) and the direction is θ = tan⁻¹(vy/vx) below the horizontal.
ai.29: Substitution: vx = 20.4788 m s⁻¹; vy = 14.3394 + (−9.8 × 2.9264) = −14.3394 m s⁻¹ (i.e. 14.3 m s⁻¹ downward); v = √(20.4788² + 14.3394²); θ = tan⁻¹(14.3394 ÷ 20.4788).
ai.30: Answer: v = 25.0 m s⁻¹ at 35.0° below the horizontal. The landing speed matches the launch speed exactly, and the landing angle mirrors the launch angle — a neat confirmation of the symmetry of a projectile launched and landing at the same height.
ai.31: Key idea Solve a projectile by axes: resolve the launch velocity into ux and uy; lean on the vertical equations (with a = g) to find the time and the height; use R = uxt for the range; then recombine vx and vy with Pythagoras and trigonometry for the final velocity. The time of flight is the one thread linking the two axes.
ai.32: Trap The maximum height arrives when the vertical velocity hits zero — not when the speed hits zero. At the top of the arc the horizontal velocity ux is still fully intact, so the ball is very much still moving, and its speed there is ux, not zero. Setting the full speed to zero at the top is a classic slip.
ai.33: Trap Carry the unrounded intermediate values right through the calculation and round only at the very end. Feed in a value you’ve already trimmed to three significant figures — say, using uy = 14.3 m s⁻¹ in the time equation — and the rounding error piles up, nudging your reported time, range and height in that final significant figure.
ai.34: Everything above quietly ignores air resistance, which is a wonderfully good approximation for dense, slow, compact objects on short flights. But for light objects, high speeds or long flights, air resistance is no longer something you can wave away — and the VCE study asks only for a qualitative description of what it does.
ai.35: Air resistance is a force that always fights the motion — it points exactly opposite to the velocity at every instant. And because the velocity hugs the path tangentially and keeps swinging direction, the air-resistance force swings around with it throughout the flight, growing stronger as the speed climbs. It’s anything but a constant force, which is precisely why the tidy constant-acceleration equations stop working.
ai.36: Here’s the qualitative story of what follows. Air resistance bleeds energy out of the projectile, so both the maximum height and the range shrink compared with the ideal parabola. The path turns lopsided: the way down is steeper than the way up, so the projectile covers less horizontal ground on the descent than on the climb. The horizontal velocity no longer holds steady — it tails off through the whole flight, because air resistance has a horizontal component shoving back against the horizontal motion. The landing speed comes in below the launch speed, and the projectile thumps down at a steeper angle than it set off.
ai.37: As a falling object picks up speed, the upward air-resistance force swells until it just matches the weight. At that moment the net force is zero, the acceleration is zero, and the object cruises on at a constant terminal velocity. A projectile sent off on a long flight drifts toward this limiting vertical speed rather than speeding up forever. The qualitative picture is summarised in Figure 1.3.
ai.38: Trap Air resistance doesn’t act vertically only. It opposes the whole velocity, so it carries a horizontal component too — which is exactly why the horizontal velocity no longer stays constant and the range comes up short. An answer that holds the horizontal velocity fixed while “adding drag” to the vertical motion alone has it wrong.
ai.39: Connects Terminal velocity is the velocity at which the resistive force balances the weight — the very same force-balance reasoning behind a charged particle settling to constant speed in a viscous medium, and behind the energy-dissipation analyses running throughout this area of study.
